evidence_quotes.quote: protections for video games that consumers have acquired
Just as the Act aims to curb predatory monetization and subscription traps

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, Id like to respectfully ask that the Digital Fairness Act include protections for video games that consumers have acquired, whether through direct purchase, subscriptions, or giveaways. Specifically, there should be safeguards to prevent games from being permanently disabled or made unplayable after release. This issue affects a growing number of players across the EU and beyond. Many games today are tied to online servers, and when those are shut down, access is lost entirely. It doesnt matter if the game was paid for or acquired for free. When its gone, its gone. Unlike books or movies, where ownership or access usually means permanence, video games can simply vanish. I experienced this myself with a game called Dead Star, developed by Armature Studio. It launched in 2016 and was removed from sale and made unplayable just seven months later when the servers were closed. There was no option to host private servers or play peer-to-peer. The entire game was lost. It wasn't a major title from a big publisher, but it mattered to the people who loved it. I dont blame the developers for their financial limits, but there should have been a way for us, the players, to preserve what we had. As a consumer, I feel powerless. Theres no legal framework ensuring I can even attempt to keep playing a game I once had access to. That doesnt seem fair, especially when the solution could be as simple as requiring publishers to offer offline modes, peer-to-peer support, or the tools to preserve these games before ending official support. This isnt about blocking innovation or interfering with development. Its about giving consumers a reasonable guarantee that the digital products they access wont disappear without recourse. Just as the Act aims to curb predatory monetization and subscription traps, this too is a matter of digital fairness. Thank you for your time and for considering this growing concern.
